Lecture 1 covers why ports matter, engineering perspective, terminal handling logic, and Indonesian port context. A strong study strategy is to compare dry bulk, general cargo, container, and liquid bulk terminals based on flow logic, equipment, and KPIs.

Lecture 2 focuses on conceptual port layout, functional zoning, wave exposure, and sedimentation risk. Prioritize safe entrance alignment, protected basin design, and practical cargo flow between berth, storage, and gate connections.
Use the Attendance page to fill the embedded attendance form, then review the attendance summary table provided on the same page.
The Resources page includes lecture PDFs, reading materials, and external references such as PIANC and UNCTAD. Use those references for technical depth and current maritime context.

Breakwater design should connect environmental loading (wave climate, water level, current) with stability checks and operational objectives, such as creating acceptable wave conditions inside the port basin.

Quay walls, fenders, and mooring systems are a linked system: quay provides structural support, fenders absorb berthing energy, and mooring controls vessel position under operational and environmental loads.

Recommended study plan: 1) review lecture notes and key terms, 2) map each terminal type to flow and KPI, 3) practice explaining one Indonesian port case, 4) solve one conceptual layout exercise with assumptions and trade-offs.

BOR means Berth Occupancy Ratio, the percentage of time a berth is occupied over a period. A common expression is BOR = (berth time used / berth time available) x 100%. Very high BOR can indicate congestion and vessel waiting risk.

Turnaround time is the total time a vessel spends in port from arrival to departure. It reflects operational efficiency and is influenced by waiting time, berth productivity, and cargo handling performance.

Port types include container, dry bulk, liquid bulk, general cargo, Ro-Ro, and multipurpose ports. By role, ports are often classified as gateway ports (serving hinterland demand) and transshipment hubs (relay nodes between shipping routes).

Main terminal types are: container terminal (TEU-oriented), dry bulk terminal (unloader-conveyor-stockyard flow), liquid bulk terminal (pipeline-tank systems), general cargo terminal (break-bulk handling), and Ro-Ro terminal (ramp-based vehicle flow).

River/estuary ports commonly face sedimentation and channel siltation, requiring recurring maintenance dredging. Other risks include variable currents/water levels, navigation constraints, and flood-related disruptions.

Sea/coastal ports are more exposed to wave loading, storm surge, and berth downtime during rough conditions. Key engineering risks include breakwater stability, overtopping, scour, and coastal erosion impacts on marine structures.

River ports are typically constrained by sedimentation and channel maintenance, while sea ports are more constrained by wave climate and storm exposure. River-port strategy emphasizes dredging reliability; sea-port strategy emphasizes protection layout and structural resilience.

Multipurpose terminals are common where cargo mix is diverse and demand is variable. They offer flexibility across cargo types, but usually trade off peak productivity compared with fully specialized terminals.

Maintenance dredging is a recurring operational cost, especially in estuary ports. Long-term port competitiveness should include dredging frequency, disposal strategy, and channel reliability impacts in OPEX planning.
